chunks.title: Course Description / Special Course Designation
chunks.content:
This class is an overview of computer networks with a focus on Internet protocols and high-performance coding in Windows.

None

chunks.title: Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Dmitri Loguinov
Email: dmitri@tamu.edu
Office: 209 PETR
Phone: 979-845-0512
Office Hours
TR 1-2pm

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: CSCE 315 (programming studio), CSCE 313 (computer systems), CSCE 312 (computer organization), CSCE 221 (data structures and algorithms), and most importantly fluent knowledge of C/C++.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content: At the end of the semester, the students will obtain experience with protocol design, Internet operation, C/C++ network APIs, and multi-threading in Windows.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials / Learning Resources
chunks.content:
This material Is: Recommended
Computer Networking
ISBN: 978-0132856201
Authors: James F. Kurose, Keith W. Ross
Publication Date: 2013-01-01
Edition: 6th

Course website: http://irl.cse.tamu.edu/courses/463/
Piazza: http://piazza.com/tamu/fall2025/csce463

chunks.title: Grading Policy / Additional Course Information (Submission Requirements)
chunks.content:
A: 90-100%, B: 80-89%, C: 70-79%, D: 60-69%, F: 0-59%

| Assignment | Qty | Format | Percent of final grade |
|---|---|---|---|
| Homework | 4 | Implementation and report | 40% (10% each) |
| Midterms | 3 | Closed-book | 45% (15% each) |
| Quizzes | 3 | Closed-book | 15% (5% each) |

All homework submissions must compile in Visual Studio 2022 in both debug and release mode. Solutions with missing files, incorrect project options, or dependency on non-standard libraries (e.g., boost) will not be graded.
Each homework must be accompanied by a written report (if there are multiple parts in the homework, the report applies only to the last part) describing your implementation and showing the performance analysis requested in the handout. Write in as much detail as possible, explain the various observations, and comment on the sanity of obtained results.
This course assumes independent work on each assignment. All submissions must be original and developed from scratch. At no time can you receive aid from external sources (e.g., current students, prior students, material found online, Al tools). The only exceptions to this policy are: sample code provided by the instructor on the course website, Microsoft help pages, and any interaction with the TA/instructor.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
The following schedule will be in effect:

| Lecture # | Title | Topic |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Preliminaries 1 | Syllabus, Visual Studio |
| 2 | Preliminaries 2 | Sockets, threads, synchronization |
| 3 | Introduction 1 | Internet structure |
| 4 | Introduction 2 | Delay and loss |
| 5 | Application layer 1 | Application types, HTTP |
| 6 | Quiz 1 | Chapter 1 |
| 7 | Application layer 2 | Caching, cookies, FTP, email |
| 8 | Application layer 3 | DNS basics |
| 9 | Application layer 4 | DNS vulnerabilities |
| 10 | Midterm 1 | Chapters 1-2, homework #1 |
| 11 | Application layer 5 | Other DNS uses, P2P |
| 12 | Transport layer 1 | Overview, multiplexing, UDP |
| 13 | Transport Layer 2 | Reliable data transfer, stop & wait |
| 14 | Quiz 2 | Chapter 2, parts of 3 |
| 15 | Transport Layer 3 | Go-back-N, Selective Repeat |
| 16 | Transport Layer 4 | Timeouts, flow control, fast retx |
| 17 | Transport Layer 5 | Congestion control |
| 18 | Midterm 2 | Chapter 3, homework #2 |
| 19 | Transport Layer 6 | TCP modeling, fairness |
| 20 | Network Layer 1 | Introduction |
| 21 | Network Layer 2 | Switching, IP header |
| 22 | Quiz 3 | Chapter 4 |
| 23 | Network Layer 3 | NAT, link-state routing |
| 24 | Network Layer 4 | Distance vector, RIP, OSPF |
| 25 | Network Layer 5 | BGP, multicast |
| 26 | Data-link Layer 1 | Error detection/correction, CSMA |
| 27 | Data-link Layer 2 | Ethernet, hubs/switches |
| 28 | Midterm 3 | Chapters 4-5, homework #3-4 |

chunks.title: Late Work Policy / Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

Homework submissions are due at 10 am. Late homework is acceptable with a penalty of 20% of the full grade per day (no points after 5 days). Delays are rounded up to the nearest day. Example #1: your homework scores 76 points out of 100, but is 28 hours late. Your score is then reduced by 40 points and becomes 36. Example #2: your part1 submission scores 19 out of 25 pts, but is 1 day late. The reduction is then 5 pts, which brings the grade down to 14.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: Any usage of AI, copilot, or LLMs is prohibited. Violations will be treated as cheating and will result in an F*.

chunks.title: CSCE 612 | Applied Networks and Distributed Processing | Fall 2025
chunks.content:
Instructor: Dmitri Loguinov | dmitri@tamu.edu
Meets: TR 11:10AM-12:25PM | HRBB 113
Topics: high-performance coding, C/C++ network APIs, multi-threading, Sockets, threads, synchronization, Go-back-N, Selective Repeat, fast retx, Congestion control, TCP modeling, fairness, Switching, IP header, NAT, link-state routing, Distance vector, RIP, OSPF, BGP, multicast, Error detection/correction, CSMA, Ethernet, hubs/switches, Internet protocols, protocol design, Internet operation, Internet structure, Delay and loss, Application types, HTTP, Caching, cookies, FTP, email, DNS basics, DNS vulnerabilities, P2P, Overview, multiplexing, UDP, Reliable data transfer, stop & wait, Timeouts, flow control, computer networks, computer systems, computer organization, data structures and algorithms, programming studio, Syllabus
Tools: Visual Studio 2022, C/C++
Prerequisites: CSCE 315, CSCE 313, CSCE 312, CSCE 221, fluent knowledge of C/C++
